author: @BezBog
text:
That was an excellent talk!

Looking forward to your next lectures, especially the ones focusing on the supercapacitors. I come from a small town that used to be full of manufacturing facilities that are all closed-up now and I see great opportunities to start something new and meaningful using graphene.

Thanks once again!

author: @centraflow1
text: Very well done Robert. I'm still working on the bottom up approach I told you about, but in relation to hydrogen cells. Certain personal matters have slowed this down but the basics are there, this combines the capacitor aspect along with electrolysis of water in a highly efficient way, then recombining the oxygen and hydrogen molecule (fuel cell) for the generation of power without heat loss to the environment. It is a system which happens all at the same time in the same cell, using electrons to do work twice over before being lost in the return circuit. Electrode base material is consumed, but cheap, giving cheap energy, graphene is the reactor and auto replaced. Keep up your good work.

replies.text: nope - no good at all - well it'd be ok for the back electrode  - but rubbish for the front electrode, incidentally graphene oxide is god as a layer between the electrolyte and front electrode as it promotes hole development

replies.author: @filmmakershelp
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
Let me know what you need.
I will gladly help.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - how do you suggest we go about this?

replies.author: @filmmakershelp
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
We could share a dropbox folder with the footage and I can work from there.
I post a private version of the video for you to review and give notes, once finished I will load it back into dropbox and you have the file to load where you like.
I can make some title graphics etc. to dress things up a bit too, if you like.
